title: Slice 7 declared 'bootstrap test PASSED' against the post-#40 SDK substrate after exercising it via curl from the container, while the homepage UI on production was simultaneously broken with a class of bugs the curl path could not surface; the substrate-level verification was technically correct but did not constitute outcome verification
content: The Slice 7 test sequence ran end-to-end against ams.klappy.dev and verified: SDK-shaped initialize response, operator-instructions pass-through, prebound conversation context, ams_create_conversation returning a magic_link, ams_join with zero arguments on the magic-link route, ams_send acceptance, GET /mcp SSE leg holding open without 404, and ams_recv long-poll completing cleanly. Every assertion was true. None of those assertions exercised the homepage's mcpInitialize() function — the function that actually drives the live UI — and the homepage's mcpInitialize did not send the Authorization header, so production sessions were created with account_id=undefined. The SDK's McpAgent binds account context at session creation and does not refresh it from subsequent tools/call requests, so every Mint click on the homepage failed with 'invalid_credential: Authorization bearer required' regardless of any subsequent fix in mcpToolCall. Operator surfaced the failure via two screenshots from ams.truthkit.ai (09:27 'The string did not match the expected pattern' = WebKit error from r.json() on SSE-framed response, fixed in PR #44; 09:39 'invalid_credential: Authorization bearer required for ams_create_conversation' = the auth-on-init bug that needed PR #45 to fix). The curl path did not exercise mcpInitialize, did not exercise WebKit's r.json() behavior on SSE, and did not exercise the SDK's session-bound auth binding. Substrate verification at curl-altitude is structurally incapable of catching homepage-altitude bugs because the homepage IS the substrate's first-and-only consumer for these failure modes.

title: The validation principle was already encoded in AGENTS.md §'Working Principles' ('Validate before declaring done — Run oddkit_validate with artifact references before any complete claim') and the agent had read it earlier in the session; the Slice 7 failure was not an absence of guidance but a failure to follow guidance that exists, with the curl path being structurally tempting because it is fast and always available from the agent's terminal
content: AGENTS.md line 213 explicitly says 'Validate before declaring done. Run oddkit_validate with artifact references before any complete claim.' The agent had AGENTS.md in its working context and had referenced the same section's mode-discipline guidance multiple times across earlier slices. The Slice 7 'PASS' declaration was made anyway, with the curl trace passed as the artifact. The structural temptation is mechanical: substrate verification is fast (single curl per assertion, runs in the agent's existing terminal session, results visible immediately as JSON), exhaustive-feeling (every protocol surface can be hit individually), and always available (no browser automation, no headless rendering, no network capture, no screenshots to review). Outcome verification is slower (must spin up Playwright, drive a real browser, capture network traffic, assert visual or DOM state), feels less rigorous to the agent because the assertions are higher-level, and requires more setup. The 'just this once' reasoning is the failure mode — and absent a runnable artifact in the repo that fails the build when the outcome is broken, 'just this once' wins every time.

title: The corrective pattern is to make the outcome itself a runnable artifact that lives in the repo — a script or test that exercises the actual user-observable behavior, fails loudly on regression, and is checked into version control alongside the code it verifies; this aligns with the upstream ODD principle and instantiates it concretely for AMS, replacing the abstract rule 'Validate before declaring done' with a concrete artifact ('node scripts/validate-homepage-mint.js') that another agent or a future you can run unchanged
content: The new artifact is scripts/validate-homepage-mint.js (Playwright-driven, fetches live homepage HTML, serves locally with ORIGIN overridden to AMS_URL, drives Mint click, captures all /v1/accounts and /mcp network traffic, asserts magic_link populates and zero error frames appear, exits 1 on regression, persists screenshot to disk for human review). It demonstrably FAILS against current production (with the bug still present) and demonstrably PASSES against the patched-HTML preview, which is the load-bearing property — a validator that does not fail on the broken state is ceremonial, not real. Operator named this in their next-turn message: 'What you just did was the original intent [of ODD]. We need to change our workflows and practices in this repo to ensure our fiasco just now doesn't keep happening.' The pattern: when an outcome lacks a runnable artifact, author one before declaring done; when the artifact already exists, run it before declaring done; when the artifact would have caught the regression but wasn't run, that is the failure mode — not a missing artifact. Prior art for the static-check version of this pattern: scripts/check-homepage-architectural-claims.mjs (D0013 enforcement, exits 1 on architectural-claim regression). The runtime version is the natural extension.

title: Author canon/constraints/outcome-verification-via-runnable-artifact as a tier-2 constraint that codifies the rule for AMS: any change with a runtime-observable outcome must have a runnable artifact in the repo that exercises that outcome end-to-end, with a clear pass/fail signal, before completion is claimed
content: The constraint specifies: (1) what counts as a runnable artifact (Playwright/Selenium/Cypress driving real browsers; Vitest/Jest integration tests booting the Worker; bash scripts composing curl+jq+diff against deployed environments; TS scripts importing public exports and asserting on results; any of the above wrapped in a Makefile or package.json target). (2) What does NOT count (reading the diff, tsc passing alone, curl tests of substrate when the change ships in UI, deploying-and-watching, asking the operator to test, citing past-tense bootstrap tests without a current runnable check, unit tests passing alone when the change has runtime/integration outcome). (3) Where existing artifacts live (scripts/check-homepage-architectural-claims.mjs and scripts/validate-homepage-mint.js, with the docstring of each naming its canon authority). (4) The escape hatch (if a runnable artifact genuinely cannot be authored, the change ships under a 'Verification Status: Outcome not verified by runnable artifact' flag, names the obstacle, opens a follow-up issue, and requires explicit operator waiver — making unverified changes loud, not frictionless). (5) Risks and mitigations (ceremonial validators rejected by 'must demonstrably fail on broken state' rule; drift mitigated by docstring-named canon authority; flakiness pain mitigated by targeting deployed surfaces over local-dev; happy-path-only coverage extended over time as failure modes surface). The constraint references upstream klappy://docs/odd/outcomes-driven-development as the principle this instantiates and AGENTS.md §'Working Principles' as the abstract rule it makes concrete.

title: Amend AGENTS.md §'AMS-Specific Discipline' to add the new constraint to the explicit list of overlay constraints that govern any change to this repo, and amend README.md to document the validator-running pattern in a new 'Local Development' section so contributors discover the pattern without having to read AGENTS.md first
content: The AGENTS.md amendment adds one bullet to the existing list (alongside D0007 spec-as-locking, D0008 horizon-as-constraint, D0011 multi-host-cname, permanent-non-goals, vodka-architecture-applied) referencing the new constraint URI and naming the failure mode it closes. The README.md addition is a small section (~10 lines) that says 'before declaring a change done, run the validator(s) for any surface the change touches' and gives the literal commands for the two existing scripts. The README addition is intentionally short — it is a pointer to discipline, not a substitute for the constraint itself. The CLAUDE.md file is a 7-line pointer and does not require amendment; the substantive guidance lives in AGENTS.md and the canon constraint.

title: The homepage helper-function bug chain (handroll → patch → SDK migration → patch → patch the patch) was a steering pattern; code generation is cheap and the operator-attention cost of accumulated patches exceeds the cost of a fresh implementation, so the right move was to throw out the helpers and write a minimal MCP client against the SDK fresh, not to keep patching the migrated handrolled-style helpers
content: The sequence: (1) Day-2 shipped a hand-rolled MCP wrapper at /mcp; (2) PR #34 added bug-bot fixes to the handroll; (3) D0024 + PR #40 swapped the substrate to Cloudflare's agents/mcp McpAgent SDK but left the homepage's mcpInitialize and mcpToolCall as the same hand-rolled helper-function style they had been against the handroll wrapper; (4) PR #43 (Cursor) and PR #44 (Claude) added an SSE parser and threaded session-id from initialize when WebKit's r.json() failed against the SDK's text/event-stream responses; (5) PR #45 added the Authorization header to mcpInitialize after empirical testing revealed the SDK's McpAgent binds account context at session creation; (6) Cursor Agent's followup commit a443ba7 narrowed the validator's error-frame detection. Each of (3)–(6) was a patch-on-patch where a fresh implementation would have been smaller. The fresh implementation: 'mcpInitialize is fetch with Authorization, parse SSE if Content-Type is text/event-stream, capture mcp-session-id, return parsed body; mcpToolCall is the same with the captured session id added; that is the entire MCP client surface the homepage needs.' That implementation, written from a blank file with the SDK's actual response shape in mind, would have been ~25 lines and would not have inherited the WebKit r.json() assumption from the handroll era. Instead the code path accumulated five compounding patches across five PRs over six hours, each reducing the surface but none erasing the inherited posture. Operator framed this as 'steering vs fresh iteration': in steering mode the agent treats existing code as a constraint and works around it; in fresh-iteration mode the agent treats the outcome as the constraint and writes the code that achieves it. Code generation is cheap; operator-attention recovery from compounding patches is expensive. The discipline lesson is parallel to but distinct from the validation-discipline lesson: validation discipline ensures the agent KNOWS when an outcome is broken; fresh-iteration discipline ensures the agent's REPAIR strategy doesn't accumulate debt that compounds the original failure. Both deserve canon encoding; this learning row captures the second lesson and flags it for follow-up promotion (potentially as a constraint titled 'fresh-iteration-over-steering' or as an extension to klappy://canon/methods/borrow-bend-break-beget-build's Beget option). Not encoded in this PR's canon constraint because that constraint is scoped to outcome-verification; conflating two distinct lessons would dilute both.

title: Future changes to AMS that touch a runtime-observable outcome MUST be verified by a runnable artifact in the repo before completion is claimed; substrate-level verification (curl, tsc, type checks, unit tests) is necessary but not sufficient when the change has a runtime outcome; declaring 'PASS' or 'verified' or 'works' without a runnable artifact that exercises the actual outcome is a violation of canon
content: Verification path for this constraint going forward: (1) before authoring a change that touches a runtime surface, identify the runnable artifact that will verify it; if none exists, author one as part of the change. (2) Run the artifact before declaring done; capture its output (network trace, screenshot, exit code) as the proof. (3) If the artifact passes, the change is verified; if it fails, the change is not done. (4) The proof is referenced in the journal entry recording the change — not just claimed in chat. (5) When operator surfaces a regression that should have been caught by an existing artifact, the artifact is extended to cover the regression as part of the fix PR. The constraint binds Claude (and any other coding agent operating on this repo) under the existing AGENTS.md §'Working Principles' authority chain — 'Validate before declaring done' is the abstract rule; this constraint provides the concrete mechanism. Violation pattern detected: declaring done after substrate-only verification is presumed a violation; the rebuttal is producing a passing-runnable-artifact reference. 'I tested via curl and it worked' does not rebut. 'I ran scripts/validate-X.js and it exited 0' does rebut.

title: Production validation proof captured by running scripts/validate-homepage-mint.js against ams.truthkit.ai after PR #45 deploys; the validator exits 0 with a populated magic_link, zero error frames, and a clean network trace showing auth+session threading on every /mcp call; this run constitutes the canonical proof that the homepage Mint flow is restored on production and that the new validator pattern works end-to-end as a verification artifact
content: Verified at 2026-05-05T15:11Z against ams.truthkit.ai (post-merge of PR #45 commit a66b35e + Cursor Agent's validator cleanup commit a443ba7). Validator command: 'node scripts/validate-homepage-mint.js'. Exit code: 0. Verbatim output:  AMS_URL: https://ams.truthkit.ai | local server up on http://127.0.0.1:8765/ | clicked Mint, waited 8000ms for flow to settle |  AMS traffic summary: GET /healthz 200 (heartbeat); POST /v1/accounts auth=false session=none -> 201 (mint); POST /mcp auth=true session=none -> 201 (initialize, returns mcp-session-id=a08eeb6896d3...); POST /mcp auth=true session=a08eeb6896d3... -> 200 (ams_create_conversation); POST /mcp auth=true session=a08eeb6896d3... -> 200 (ams_join); GET /mcp auth=true session=a08eeb6896d3... -> 200 (SSE attach leg); GET /healthz 200 (heartbeat).  Final state: link populated to https://ams.truthkit.ai/tincan-a56x0s/conversations/calm-echo-4741?t=sOgmrkPF84Kq6Nh-oAw6zrGx2kQbVwB4fPt_9qpCmks; error frames count: 0; page errors: 0.  VERDICT: PASS.  Visual confirmation captured at /mnt/user-data/outputs/post-merge-production-validation.png — homepage shows successful Mint flow with magic_link in input, all four MCP frames in tincan log, joined event rendered, no error frames.  This run constitutes the canonical proof the homepage Mint flow is restored on production AND that the validator pattern works as designed (runs unchanged from the repo, produces machine-checkable output, asserts the outcome a human would assert by clicking Mint themselves).
